name: Tofu
recipeIngredient: 2 cups washed soybeans (preferably a large, low oil variety such as Vintons (Pinetree Seeds), but any will do)
recipeInstructions:
1. Cover the beans with water and let soak for several hours until they split easily and have flat surfaces. The beans should be pale in color; avoid sunken yellow spots by soaking longer if necessary.
2. Drain the beans.
3. Bring 6 quarts of water to a boil in one pan or kettle, and another 16 quarts to boil in a large deep pan.
4. Turn off the heat.
5. Blend 1 cup of beans with 1½ cups boiling water from the smaller pan until well blended. The resulting mix resembles wet cream of wheat.
6. Continue blending the beans 1 cup at a time and add the mixture to the larger pan, stirring constantly.
7. Use a tofu bag (made from old flour sacks or two non-terry cloth kitchen towels sewn together) lined with moistened fabric over a rack above another large pan filled with water.
8. Pour the mixture into the sack and drain, pressing to squeeze out all of the soymilk.
9. You will now have soymilk and okara (the solids in the bag). Set the okara aside or freeze it for later use.
10. Put the soymilk back on the stove over medium-low heat and bring it to a simmer without burning it. Stir occasionally to prevent sticking and scorching.
11. Bring the soymilk to a simmering boil, then continue boiling for 7 minutes. Skim off any foam that forms on top if desired; this is called "yuba" and can be fried in butter or oil for a chicken skin-like texture.
12. While heating the soymilk, prepare the coagulant: mix 2 teaspoons of Epsom salts with 1 cup of warm water, or use 2 tablespoons of nigari mixed with 1 cup of warm water, or 2 tablespoons of lemon juice in 1 cup of warm water.
13. Remove the soymilk from heat and sprinkle about half of the coagulant gently over the soymilk, cutting through it without stirring.
14. Let stand for a few minutes until curds form. Sprinkle the remaining coagulant in the same manner and repeat if necessary until you have white curds separated from pale yellow whey with no creaminess left.
15. Place the tofu pressing box (a 6"x8" wooden box with holes on each side or a colander) on a rack over another large pan, lined with moistened cloth.
16. Drain as much whey as possible without disturbing the curds by gently ladling them into the pressing box.
17. Cover the tofu in the box with the cloth, place a weight (3-5 pounds) on top, and let sit for 20 minutes.
18. Remove the cloth and store the tofu in water in the refrigerator or freeze it for an interesting texture change. Enjoy your homemade tofu!
